The **Tiger II** was a [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") [heavy tank](/wiki/Heavy_tank "Heavy tank") of the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The final official German designation was ***Panzerkampfwagen* Tiger *Ausf*. B**,[[a]](#cite_note-9) often shortened to **Tiger B**.[[9]](#cite_note-J&D16-10) The ordnance inventory designation was *[Sd.Kfz.](/wiki/List_of_Sd.Kfz._designations "List of Sd.Kfz. designations")* 182.[[9]](#cite_note-J&D16-10) (*Sd.Kfz.* 267 and 268 for command vehicles). It was also known informally as the ***Königstiger***[[9]](#cite_note-J&D16-10) ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language") for [Bengal tiger](/wiki/Bengal_tiger "Bengal tiger"), lit. 'King Tiger').[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) [Allied](/wiki/Allied_forces_(World_War_II) "Allied forces (World War II)") soldiers often called it the **King Tiger** or **Royal Tiger**.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The Tiger II was the successor to the [Tiger I](/wiki/Tiger_I "Tiger I"), combining the latter's thick armour with the [armour sloping](/wiki/Armour_sloping "Armour sloping") used on the [Panther](/wiki/Panther_tank "Panther tank") [medium tank](/wiki/Medium_tank "Medium tank"). It was the costliest German tank to produce at the time. The tank weighed almost 70 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne") and was protected by 100 to 185 mm (3.9 to 7.3 in) of armour to the front.[[13]](#cite_note-J&Darmor-14) It was armed with the long barrelled (71 calibres) [8.8 cm KwK 43](/wiki/8.8_cm_KwK_43 "8.8 cm KwK 43") anti-tank cannon.[[b]](#cite_note-15) The chassis was also the basis for the *[Jagdtiger](/wiki/Jagdtiger "Jagdtiger")* turretless *[Jagdpanzer](/wiki/Jagdpanzer "Jagdpanzer")* [anti-tank vehicle](/wiki/Tank_destroyer "Tank destroyer").[[14]](#cite_note-Schneider19-16)

The Tiger II was issued to [heavy tank battalions](/wiki/German_heavy_tank_battalion "German heavy tank battalion") of the [Army](/wiki/German_Army_(1935%E2%80%931945) "German Army (1935–1945)") and the *[Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS")*. It was first used in combat by [503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/503rd_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion") during the Allied [invasion of Normandy](/wiki/Operation_Overlord "Operation Overlord") on 11 July 1944;[[15]](#cite_note-17) on the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)"), the first unit to be outfitted with the Tiger II was the [501st Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/501st_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "501st Heavy Panzer Battalion").[[16]](#cite_note-18) Due to heavy Allied bombing, only 492 were produced.

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Development")]

Development started in 1937 with a design contract awarded to [Henschel](/wiki/Henschel_%26_Son "Henschel & Son"). Another design contract followed in 1939, given to [Porsche](/wiki/Ferdinand_Porsche "Ferdinand Porsche").[[17]](#cite_note-Jentz_&_Doyle_1993,_p._3-19) Both prototypes used the same turret design from [Krupp](/wiki/Krupp "Krupp"). The main differences were in the hull, transmission, suspension and automotive features.[[17]](#cite_note-Jentz_&_Doyle_1993,_p._3-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Chambois1.jpg/250px-Chambois1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chambois1.jpg)

[Supreme commander of the allied forces in Europe](/wiki/Supreme_Headquarters_Allied_Expeditionary_Force "Supreme Headquarters Allied Expeditionary Force") General [Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") walks by an overturned Tiger II destroyed in the [Falaise pocket](/wiki/Falaise_pocket "Falaise pocket") August 1944

The Henschel version used a conventional hull design with sloped armour resembling the layout of the Panther tank. It had a rear-mounted engine and used nine steel-tired, eighty-centimetre-diameter overlapping road wheels per side with internal springing, mounted on [transverse torsion bars](/wiki/Torsion_bar_suspension "Torsion bar suspension"), in a similar manner to the original Henschel-designed Tiger I. To simplify maintenance, however, the wheels were only overlapping *without* being interleaved—the full *Schachtellaufwerk* rubber-rimmed road-wheel system that had been in use on nearly all German [half-tracks](/wiki/Half-track "Half-track") used the interleaved design, later inherited by the Tiger I[[18]](#cite_note-20) and Panther.

The Porsche hull designs included a rear-mounted turret and a mid-mounted engine. The suspension was the same as on the *[Elefant](/wiki/Elefant "Elefant")* tank destroyer. This had six road wheels per side mounted in paired [bogies](/wiki/Bogies "Bogies") sprung with short longitudinal torsion bars that were integral to the wheel pair; this saved internal space and facilitated repairs. One Porsche version had a gasoline-electric drive (fundamentally identical to a [diesel-electric transmission](/wiki/Diesel-electric_transmission "Diesel-electric transmission"), only using a gasoline-fueled engine as the [prime mover](/wiki/Prime_mover_(locomotive) "Prime mover (locomotive)")), similar to a [gasoline-electric hybrid](/wiki/Gasoline-electric_hybrid "Gasoline-electric hybrid") but without a storage battery; two separate [drivetrains](/wiki/Drivetrain "Drivetrain") in parallel, one per side of the tank, each consisting of a hybrid drive train; gasoline engine–[electric generator](/wiki/Electric_generator "Electric generator")–electric motor–drive [sprocket](/wiki/Sprocket "Sprocket"). This method of propulsion had been used on the rejected [Tiger (P)](/wiki/VK_45.01_(P) "VK 45.01 (P)") design, which had been rebuilt as *Elefant*, and in some US designs and was put into production in the French World War I era [Saint-Chamond](/wiki/Saint-Chamond_(tank) "Saint-Chamond (tank)") tank and post-World War I [Char 2C](/wiki/Char_2C "Char 2C"). The Porsche suspension components were later used on a few of the later *[Jagdtiger](/wiki/Jagdtiger "Jagdtiger")* tank destroyers. Another proposal was to use hydraulic drives; Dr. Porsche's unorthodox designs gathered little favour.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Design")]

[![A tank turret with a front face which curves up and down. The sides are slanted vertically and curved laterally.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Munster_Koenigstiger_Porscheturm_Modell_detail.jpg/250px-Munster_Koenigstiger_Porscheturm_Modell_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Munster_Koenigstiger_Porscheturm_Modell_detail.jpg)

A model depicting the curved front of the first version of the Krupp turret (erroneously called "Porsche turret")[[20]](#cite_note-:0-22)

Henschel won the design contract, and all Tiger IIs were produced by the firm.[[21]](#cite_note-23) Two turret designs were used in production vehicles. The initial design is often misleadingly called the "Porsche" turret due to the misbelief that it was designed by Porsche for their [Tiger II](/wiki/VK_4502_(P) "VK 4502 (P)") prototype; in fact it was the initial Krupp design for both prototypes.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-22) This turret had a rounded front and steeply sloped sides, with a difficult-to-manufacture curved bulge on the turret's left side to accommodate the commander's cupola. Fifty early turrets were mounted to Henschel hulls and used in action. In December 1943 the more common "production" turret, sometimes erroneously called the "Henschel" turret, was simplified with a significantly thicker flat face (which eliminated the [shot trap](/wiki/Shot_trap "Shot trap") caused by the curved face of the earlier turret), and less-steeply sloped sides, which avoided the need for a bulge for the commander's cupola, and added additional room for ammunition storage.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

[![A tank turret with an almost square, flat, vertical face, the sides are almost vertical, and curve laterally only slightly.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-680-8282A-09%2C_Budapest%2C_Panzersoldaten_in_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_II%29.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-680-8282A-09%2C_Budapest%2C_Panzersoldaten_in_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_II%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-680-8282A-09,_Budapest,_Panzersoldaten_in_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_II).jpg)

The angular front of the "production turret" designed by [Krupp](/wiki/Krupp "Krupp") (erroneously called "Henschel turret")[[20]](#cite_note-:0-22) taken during [Operation Panzerfaust](/wiki/Operation_Panzerfaust "Operation Panzerfaust") in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), 15 October 1944. The rough [Zimmerit](/wiki/Zimmerit "Zimmerit") coating is evident, used to prevent magnetic mines from adhering to the tank's armour.

The turrets were designed to mount the 8.8 cm KwK 43 L/71 gun. Combined with the *Turmzielfernrohr* 9d (German "turret telescopic sight") monocular sight by Leitz, which all but a few early Tiger IIs used, it was a very accurate and deadly weapon. During practice, the estimated probability of a first-round hit on a 2 m (6 ft 7 in) high, 2.5 m (8 ft 2 in) wide target was 100 percent at 1,000 m (1,100 yd), 95–97 percent at 1,500 m (1,600 yd) and 85–87 percent at 2,000 m (2,200 yd), depending on ammunition type.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Recorded combat performance was lower, but still over 80 percent at 1,000 m, in the 60s at 1,500 m and the 40s at 2,000 m. Penetration of armoured plate inclined at 30 degrees was 202 and 132 mm (8.0 and 5.2 in) at 100 m (110 yd) and 2,000 m (2,200 yd) respectively for the [*Panzergranate* 39/43](/wiki/Panzergranate_39 "Panzergranate 39") projectile (PzGr – [armour-piercing shell](/wiki/Armor-piercing_shot_and_shell "Armor-piercing shot and shell")), and 238 and 153 mm (9.4 and 6.0 in) for the [PzGr. 40/43](/w/index.php?title=Panzergranate_40&action=edit&redlink=1 "Panzergranate 40 (page does not exist)") projectile between the same ranges.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The *Sprenggranate* 43 (SpGr) high-explosive round was available for soft targets, or the *Hohlgranate* or *Hohlgeschoss* 39 (HlGr – HEAT or [High-explosive anti-tank warhead](/wiki/High-explosive_anti-tank_warhead "High-explosive anti-tank warhead")) round, which had 90 mm (3.5 in) penetration at any range, could be used as a dual-purpose munition against soft or armoured targets.[[23]](#cite_note-J&D23-24-25)

Powered turret traverse was provided by the variable speed Boehringer-Sturm L4S hydraulic motor, which was driven from the main engine by a secondary drive shaft. A high and a low speed setting was available to the gunner via a lever on his right. The turret could be rotated 360 degrees at 6º/second in low gear independent of engine rpm, at 19º/second – the same as with the Tiger I – with the high speed setting and engine at 2000 rpm, and over 36º/second at the maximum allowable engine speed of 3,000 rpm. The direction and speed of traverse was controlled by the gunner through foot pedals, whilst a high torque low speed (useful when on slopes) or low torque high speed final gearing could be selected via a control lever near his left arm. This system allowed for very precise control of powered traverse, a light touch on the pedal resulting in a minimum traverse speed of 0.1 deg/sec (360 degrees in 60 min), unlike in most other tanks of the time (e.g. US [M4 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman "M4 Sherman") or Soviet [T-34](/wiki/T-34 "T-34") medium tanks) this allowed for fine laying of the gun without the gunner needing to use his traverse handwheel.[[24]](#cite_note-26) If power was lost, such as when the tank ran out of fuel, the turret could be slowly traversed by hand, assisted by the loader who had an additional wheel, which could manually rotate the turret at a rate of one-half a degree per each revolution of the hand crank; a 20° turret rotation required 40 full cranks of the handwheel, and to turn the turret a full 360° the gunner would be required to crank the handwheel 720 full revolutions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![The overhanging rear face of a large tank, two laterally spaced exhaust pipes protrude from mountings, pointing upwards, curving away from the vehicle at their ends.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Tiger_II_mg_7802.jpg/250px-Tiger_II_mg_7802.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiger_II_mg_7802.jpg)

Rear view showing dual exhausts

Like all German tanks, the Tiger II had a [petrol engine](/wiki/Petrol_engine "Petrol engine"); in this case the same 700 [PS](/wiki/Horsepower#PS "Horsepower") (690 hp, 515 kW) V-12 [Maybach HL 230 P30](/wiki/Maybach_HL230 "Maybach HL230") which powered the much lighter Panther and Tiger I tanks. The Tiger II was under-powered, like many other heavy tanks of World War II[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], and consumed a lot of fuel, which was in short supply for the Germans. The transmission was the Maybach OLVAR OG 40 12 16 Model B, giving eight forward gears and four reverse, which drove the steering gear. This was the Henschel L 801, a double radius design which proved susceptible to failure. Transverse torsion bar suspension supported the hull on nine axles per side. Overlapped 800 mm (31 in) diameter road wheels with rubber cushions and steel tyres rode inside the tracks.[[25]](#cite_note-J&D11-12-27) Late production Tiger Is received the same wheels, which were one of the few interchangeable parts between the two tanks.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

Like the Tiger I, each tank was issued with two sets of tracks: a normal "battle track" and a narrower "transport" version used during rail movement. The transport tracks reduced the overall width of the load and could be used to drive the tank short distances on firm ground. The crew were expected to change to normal battle tracks as soon as the tank was unloaded. Ground pressure was 0.76 kg/cm2 (10.8 psi).[[27]](#cite_note-29)

### Command variant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Command variant")]

The command variant of the Tiger II was designated *Panzerbefehlswagen* Tiger *Ausf. B*. It had two versions, *Sd.Kfz*. 267 and *Sd.Kfz*. 268. These had reduced ammunition capacity (only 63 rounds of 8.8 cm ammunition) to provide room for the extra radios and equipment,[[9]](#cite_note-J&D16-10) and had additional armour on the engine compartment. The *Sd.Kfz*. 267 was to have used *FuG* 8 and *FuG* 5 radio sets, with the most notable external changes being a two-metre-long (6.6 ft) rod antenna mounted on the turret roof and a *Sternantenne D* ("Star antenna D"), mounted on an insulated base (the 105 mm *Antennenfuß Nr. 1*), which was protected by a large armoured cylinder. This equipment was located on the rear decking in a position originally used for deep-wading equipment.[[9]](#cite_note-J&D16-10) The *Sd.Kfz*. 268 used *FuG* 7 and *FuG* 5 radios with a two-metre rod antenna mounted on the turret roof and a 1.4 metre rod antenna mounted on the rear deck.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Production")]

The Tiger II was developed late in the war and built in relatively small numbers. Orders were placed for 1,500 Tiger IIs—slightly more than the 1,347 Tiger I tanks produced—but production was heavily disrupted by Allied bombing raids.[[29]](#cite_note-Manchester68-498-31) Among others, five raids between 22 September and 7 October 1944 destroyed 95 percent of the floor area of the Henschel plant. It is estimated that this caused the loss in production of 657 Tiger IIs.[[30]](#cite_note-J&D17-32) Only 492 units were produced: one in 1943, 379 in 1944, and 112 in 1945. Full production ran from mid-1944 to the end of the war.[[2]](#cite_note-Jentz96-288-2) Each Tiger II cost 321 500 Reichsmark.[[31]](#cite_note-33) The vehicle was the costliest German tank to produce at the time.[[32]](#cite_note-34)

The Tiger II served as the basis for one production variant, the *[Jagdtiger](/wiki/Jagdtiger "Jagdtiger")* [casemated](/wiki/Casemate "Casemate") tank destroyer,[[14]](#cite_note-Schneider19-16) and a proposed [*Grille*](/wiki/Grille_(artillery) "Grille (artillery)") 17/21/30/42 self-propelled mount for heavy guns which never reached production.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Proposed upgrades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Proposed upgrades")]

The Maybach [HL234](/wiki/List_of_WWII_Maybach_engines#HL234 "List of WWII Maybach engines"), an engine born from attempting to convert the Maybach HL230 to [fuel injection](/wiki/Fuel_injection "Fuel injection"), would have increased the power from 700 to at least 800 PS (hp). In January 1945 the *Entwicklungskommission Panzer* unanimously decided that HL234 be immediately included in the engine design and procurement program. The ZF AK-7-200 gearbox was also explored as an alternative to the Maybach Olvar-B semi-automatic gearbox, but *[Waffenamt](/wiki/Waffenamt "Waffenamt")* research and development department *Wa Prüf 6* found that it offered inferior driving characteristics and so the Maybach Olvar-B was retained.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle1997144-154-35) There was also a program using the Simmering-Graz-Pauker Sla.16-cylinder diesel engine,[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle1997144-154-35) but the war's constraint on supplies and Germany's capitulation resulted in the cancellation of this program.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Krupp proposed mounting a new main weapon, the [10.5 cm](/wiki/105_mm_calibre "105 mm calibre") KwK L/68. *Wa Prüf 6* did not support this as the *Heer* had not accepted the cannon. Other suggested improvements included stabilised sights, a stabilised main gun, an [automatic ammunition feed](/wiki/Autoloader "Autoloader"), a [Carl Zeiss AG](/wiki/Carl_Zeiss_AG "Carl Zeiss AG") [stereoscopic rangefinder](/wiki/Stereoscopic_rangefinder "Stereoscopic rangefinder"), heated crew compartment, stowage for an additional 12 rounds, and an [overpressure](/wiki/Overpressure_(CBRN_protection) "Overpressure (CBRN protection)") and air filtration system to protect against [poison gas](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon"). However, these also never got beyond the proposal stage or did not enter production before the war ended.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJentzDoyle1997144-154-35)

### Reliability and mobility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Reliability and mobility")]

[![A large, turreted tank with dull yellow, green and brown wavy camouflage, on display inside Bovington museum. The tracks are wide, and the frontal armour is sloped. The long gun overhangs the bow by several meters.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Bovington_Tiger_II_grey_bg_b.jpg/250px-Bovington_Tiger_II_grey_bg_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bovington_Tiger_II_grey_bg_b.jpg)

A camouflaged Tiger II on display at Bovington Tank museum. The long gun overhangs the bow by several meters.

Early Tiger IIs proved unreliable, owing principally to leaking seals and gaskets, an overburdened drive train originally intended for a lighter vehicle, and teething problems with the final drive and steering unit, both of which had been newly designed for the Tiger II.[[37]](#cite_note-Jentz_and_Doyle_1993,_p._34-40)[[38]](#cite_note-41) The final drive unit and the double radius steering gear were initially particularly prone to failures.[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43)

The new double-link track proved to be vulnerable to sideways stresses when the tank was driving on uneven terrain, as well as causing only every other sprocket tooth to engage with the track, leading to their rapid wear and potentially damaging the final drive. The inspector general of panzer troops, [Wolfgang Thomale](/wiki/Wolfgang_Thomale "Wolfgang Thomale"), said in a briefing on November 4, 1944, "These complaints could be traced back to the new track, which, although a considerable production simplification, on the other hand entails a greater susceptibility of the Tiger." The engagement of only every second sprocket tooth was causing "sudden jerks in the final drive, which cannot withstand these blows".[[41]](#cite_note-44)

Tiger II 332 arrives at the [U.S. Army Armor and Cavalry Collection](/wiki/U.S._Army_Armor_and_Cavalry_Collection "U.S. Army Armor and Cavalry Collection"), [Fort Benning](/wiki/Fort_Benning "Fort Benning"), Georgia

Henschel's chief designer, [Erwin Aders](/wiki/Erwin_Aders "Erwin Aders"), wrote, "The failure occurred because the Tiger II went into production without considering the test results."[[42]](#cite_note-45) Lack of crew training could amplify this problem; drivers originally given only limited training on other tanks were often sent directly to operational units already on their way to the front.[[37]](#cite_note-Jentz_and_Doyle_1993,_p._34-40)

The [*Schwere Heeres Panzer Abteilung* 501](/wiki/501st_heavy_tank_battalion_(Germany) "501st heavy tank battalion (Germany)") arrived on the Eastern Front with only eight out of 45 tanks operational; these faults were mostly due to final drive failures. The first five Tiger IIs delivered to the [Panzer Lehr Division](/wiki/Panzer_Lehr_Division "Panzer Lehr Division") broke down before they could be used in combat, and were destroyed to prevent capture.[[43]](#cite_note-J&D35-46)

Henschel worked closely with crews to solve the problems, and with the introduction of modified seals, gaskets, drive train components and a new track and sprocket wheel design, as well as improved driver training and sufficient maintenance, the Tiger II could be maintained in a satisfactory operational condition.[[44]](#cite_note-47) Statistics from 15 March 1945 show reliability rates of 59 percent for the Tiger, almost equal to the 62 percent of the [Panzer IV](/wiki/Panzer_IV "Panzer IV") and better than the 48 percent of the Panther that were operational by this period.[[35]](#cite_note-J&D36-38)

The [503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/503rd_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion") report noted in a November 1944, after-action report on operations in Hungary:

> ...The battalion went into action in two battle groups with two different divisions on two different days. Provided the assault was successful in penetrating into the enemy rear, the battalion would then reunite. Both groups were extraordinarily successful. From 19–23 October 1944, 120 anti-tank guns and 19 guns were destroyed. The extremely tough and steadfast enemy ([penal battalions](/wiki/Penal_military_unit "Penal military unit")) was shaken to the core by the energetic assault and his communications to the rear thrown into total confusion by the destruction of various columns and a transport train which, in the final analysis, forced the Russian Sixth Army from the [Debrecen](/wiki/Debrecen "Debrecen") area. The total distance of about 250 kilometers covered during the operation was accomplished essentially without mechanical failure. The Tiger II proved itself extremely well, both in its armor and from a mechanical perspective. Vehicles which received up to twenty hits without becoming disabled were not uncommon. ... In summary, the Tiger II has proven itself in every way and is a weapon that the enemy fears. When the formation is used as a single, unified entity and is employed in accordance with proper tactics, it always brings decisive success.[[45]](#cite_note-48)

Notwithstanding its initial reliability problems, the Tiger II was remarkably agile for such a heavy vehicle. Contemporary German records and testing results indicate that its tactical mobility was as good as or better than most German or Allied tanks.[[46]](#cite_note-49)[[d]](#cite_note-51)

Lt Col H.A. Shields of the [American Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army")'s [66th Armored Regiment](/wiki/66th_Armored_Regiment "66th Armored Regiment") reported in 1945:

> Wherever we have seen Tiger or Panther tanks, they have not demonstrated any inferior maneuverability. Near [Pottendorf](/wiki/Pottendorf "Pottendorf"), several Royal Tiger tanks were encountered. These Royal Tigers were able to negotiate very soft ground and their tracks did not sink in soft ground as did our own.

### Combat history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Combat history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-721-0397-34%2C_Frankreich%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_II%2C_K%C3%B6nigstiger%29_crop.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-721-0397-34%2C_Frankreich%2C_Panzer_VI_%28Tiger_II%2C_K%C3%B6nigstiger%29_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-721-0397-34,_Frankreich,_Panzer_VI_(Tiger_II,_K%C3%B6nigstiger)_crop.jpg)

Tiger IIs (with the first version of the Krupp turret) on the move in France, June 1944

The first combat use of the Tiger II was by the 1st Company of the [503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/503rd_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion") (*s.H.Pz.Abt*. 503) during the [Battle of Normandy](/wiki/Operation_Overlord "Operation Overlord"), opposing the Canadian offensive [Operation Atlantic](/wiki/Operation_Atlantic "Operation Atlantic") between [Troarn](/wiki/Troarn "Troarn") and [Demouville](/wiki/Demouville "Demouville") on 18 July 1944. Two were lost in combat, while the company commander's tank became irrecoverably trapped after falling into a bomb crater created during [Operation Goodwood](/wiki/Operation_Goodwood "Operation Goodwood").[[48]](#cite_note-Schneider133-52)

On the Eastern Front, it was first used on 12 August 1944 by the [501st Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/501st_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "501st Heavy Panzer Battalion") (*s.H.Pz.Abt*. 501) resisting the [Lvov–Sandomierz Offensive](/wiki/Lvov%E2%80%93Sandomierz_Offensive "Lvov–Sandomierz Offensive"). It attacked the Soviet bridgehead over the [Vistula River](/wiki/Vistula_River "Vistula River") near [Baranów Sandomierski](/wiki/Baran%C3%B3w_Sandomierski "Baranów Sandomierski"). On the road to [Oględów](/wiki/Ogl%C4%99d%C3%B3w "Oględów"), three Tiger IIs were destroyed in an ambush by a few [T-34-85s](/wiki/T-34 "T-34").[[49]](#cite_note-53) Because these German tanks suffered ammunition explosions, which caused many crew fatalities, main gun rounds were no longer allowed to be stowed within the turret, reducing capacity to 68.[[50]](#cite_note-54) Up to fourteen Tiger IIs of the 501st were destroyed or captured in the area between 11 and 14 August to ambushes and flank attacks by both Soviet T-34-85 and [IS-2](/wiki/IS-2 "IS-2") tanks, and [ISU-122](/wiki/ISU-122 "ISU-122") assault guns in inconvenient sandy terrain. The capture of three operational Tiger IIs allowed the Soviets to conduct tests at [Kubinka](/wiki/Kubinka "Kubinka") and to evaluate its strengths and weaknesses[[51]](#cite_note-55)

[![A large tank with sloped frontal armour and a flat faced turret, by a column of marching soldiers wearing overcoats and helmets, in a wide city street. A large building to the rear shows the scars of battle.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-680-8283A-12A%2C_Budapest%2C_marschierende_Pfeilkreuzler_und_Panzer_VI.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-680-8283A-12A%2C_Budapest%2C_marschierende_Pfeilkreuzler_und_Panzer_VI.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-680-8283A-12A,_Budapest,_marschierende_Pfeilkreuzler_und_Panzer_VI.jpg)

A Tiger II of [s.H.Pz.Abt. 503](/wiki/503rd_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion") and Hungarian troops in a battle-scarred street in [Buda](/wiki/Buda "Buda")'s Castle district, October 1944

On 15 October 1944, Tiger IIs of 503rd Heavy Panzer Battalion played a crucial role during [Operation Panzerfaust](/wiki/Operation_Panzerfaust "Operation Panzerfaust"), supporting [Otto Skorzeny](/wiki/Otto_Skorzeny "Otto Skorzeny")'s troops in taking the Hungarian capital of [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), which ensured that the country remained with the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") until the end of the war. The 503rd then took part in the [Battle of Debrecen](/wiki/Battle_of_Debrecen "Battle of Debrecen"). The 503rd remained in the Hungarian theater of operations for 166 days, during which time it accounted for at least 121 Soviet tanks, 244 anti-tank guns and artillery pieces, five aircraft and a train. This was set against the loss of 25 Tiger IIs; ten were knocked out by Soviet troops and burned out, two were sent back to [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") for a factory overhaul, while thirteen were blown up by their crews for various reasons, usually to prevent them from falling into enemy hands.

The Tiger II was also used in significant numbers, distributed into four heavy panzer battalions, during the [Ardennes Offensive](/wiki/Ardennes_Offensive "Ardennes Offensive") (also known as the 'Battle of the Bulge') of December 1944.[[52]](#cite_note-56) At least 150 Tiger IIs were present, nearly a third of total production; most were lost over the course of the offensive.[[53]](#cite_note-57)

Some Tiger IIs were also present during the Soviet [Vistula–Oder](/wiki/Vistula%E2%80%93Oder_Offensive "Vistula–Oder Offensive")[[54]](#cite_note-58) and [East Prussian Offensives](/wiki/East_Prussian_Offensive "East Prussian Offensive") in January 1945,[[55]](#cite_note-59) as well as the German [Lake Balaton Offensive](/wiki/Lake_Balaton_Offensive "Lake Balaton Offensive") in Hungary in March 1945,[[56]](#cite_note-60) the [Battle of the Seelow Heights](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Seelow_Heights "Battle of the Seelow Heights") in April 1945, and the [Battle of Berlin](/wiki/Battle_of_Berlin "Battle of Berlin") at the end of the war.[[57]](#cite_note-61)

The [103rd SS Heavy Panzer Battalion](/wiki/103rd_SS_Heavy_Panzer_Battalion "103rd SS Heavy Panzer Battalion") (*s.SS Pz.Abt*. 503) claimed approximately 500 kills in the period from January to April 1945 on the Eastern Front for the loss of 45 Tiger IIs (most of which were abandoned and destroyed by their own crews after mechanical breakdowns or for lack of fuel).[[58]](#cite_note-62)

### Gun and armour performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Gun and armour performance")]

[![A head-on view of a large tank with a flat-faced turret. Its sloped bow armour is scarred with several fist-sized dents, and there is a fist-sized hole in the front of the turret](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a3/Tiger_II_punctured_in_front_turret.jpg/250px-Tiger_II_punctured_in_front_turret.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiger_II_punctured_in_front_turret.jpg)

A Tiger II with several failed penetrations in its front armour and a penetration in its turret.[[59]](#cite_note-63)

The heavy armour and powerful long-range gun gave the Tiger II an advantage against all opposing [Western Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") and [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") tanks attempting to engage it from head on. This was especially true on the Western Front where, until the arrival of the few [M26 Pershings](/wiki/M26_Pershing "M26 Pershing") in 1945 and the few [M4A3E2 Sherman](/wiki/M4_Sherman#M4A3E2 "M4 Sherman") "Jumbo" assault tanks with additional armour[[e]](#cite_note-64) that were scattered around Europe after D-Day, as well as a few late [Churchill](/wiki/Churchill_tank "Churchill tank") models,[[f]](#cite_note-65) neither the British nor US forces brought heavy tanks into service. A *Wa Prüf 1* report estimated that the Tiger II's frontal aspect was impervious to the Soviet 122 mm [D-25T](/wiki/D-25T "D-25T"), one of the largest calibre tank guns of the war. Soviet testing found that the frontal glacis could be destroyed only by firing 3–4 shots at the weld joints from the ranges of 500–600m.[[60]](#cite_note-Tekhnika_molodezhi-66) Weld joints were found to be inferior quality to the Tiger I and Panther.[[61]](#cite_note-Tigertest-67) An R.A.C 3.d. document of February 1945 estimated that the British (76.2 mm) [QF 17-pounder gun](/wiki/Ordnance_QF_17-pounder "Ordnance QF 17-pounder"), using [armour-piercing discarding sabot](/wiki/Armour-piercing_discarding_sabot "Armour-piercing discarding sabot") shot was theoretically capable of penetrating the front of the Tiger II's turret and nose (lower front hull) at 1,100 and 1,200 yd (1,000 and 1,100 m) respectively although, given the lack of a stated angle, this was presumably at the ideal 90 degrees and in combat the Tiger II was never penetrated frontally by the QF 17-Pounder.[[62]](#cite_note-KTHT34-36-68)

As a result of its thick frontal armour, flanking manoeuvres were most often used against the Tiger II to attempt a shot at the thinner side and rear armour, giving a tactical advantage to the Tiger II in most engagements.[[63]](#cite_note-Jary274-69) Moreover, the main armament of the Tiger II was capable of knocking out any Allied tank frontally at ranges exceeding 2.5 kilometres (1.6 mi), well beyond the effective range of Allied tank guns.[[64]](#cite_note-Jary258-70)

### Soviet wartime testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Soviet wartime testing")]

During August 1944, two Tiger Ausf B tanks were captured by the Soviets near [Sandomierz](/wiki/Sandomierz "Sandomierz"), and were soon moved to the testing grounds at [Kubinka](/wiki/Kubinka "Kubinka"). During the transfer, the two tanks suffered from mechanical breakdowns. The cooling system was insufficient for the excessively hot weather, causing overheated engines and gearbox failure. The right suspension of one of the tanks had to be completely replaced, and its full functionality could not be re-established. The design of the track tensioning mechanism hadn't been completely perfected. As a result, the tension had to be adjusted after every 10–15 km. The 8.8 cm KwK 43 gave positive results in penetration and accuracy, which were on par with the 122 mm D-25T. It proved capable of passing completely through its "colleague", a Tiger Ausf B's turret at a range of 400 m. The armour of one vehicle was tested by firing at it with shells between 100 and 152 mm calibre. The welding was, despite careful workmanship, significantly worse than on similar designs. As a result, even when shells did not penetrate the armour, there was often a large amount of [spalling](/wiki/Spalling "Spalling") from the inside of the plates, which damaged the transmission and rendered the tank inoperable. Further testing showed that the armour plate was of an inferior quality to earlier German tanks such as the Tiger I and Panther. Lab testing found the plates lacked molybdenum (ascribed to a loss of supply, being replaced by vanadium), resulting in low malleability.[[61]](#cite_note-Tigertest-67)[[65]](#cite_note-71)

The expanded firing test states that the [АР](/wiki/Armor-piercing_shot_and_shell "Armor-piercing shot and shell") projectiles from the [100 mm BS-3](/wiki/100_mm_field_gun_M1944_(BS-3) "100 mm field gun M1944 (BS-3)") and [122 mm A-19](/wiki/122_mm_gun_M1931_(A-19) "122 mm gun M1931 (A-19)") gun penetrated a Tiger Ausf B's turret at ranges of 1000–1500 metres. However, the firing test against the turret front was conducted after removal of the gun and mantlet, and penetrations were close to openings such as vision slits and the gun location. The penetrations to the right gun opening occurred after previous 100 mm projectile penetration hits or armour damage.[[66]](#cite_note-72) The 100 mm BS-3 and 122 mm A-19 could also penetrate the weld joints of the front hull at ranges of 500–600 metres after 3–4 shots.[[60]](#cite_note-Tekhnika_molodezhi-66)

## Surviving vehicles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tiger_II&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Surviving vehicles")]

[![The side of a large tank with wide, wavy green and grey striped camouflage, as it drives past, the commander sitting in the cupola.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Koenigstiger_Saumur_F.jpg/250px-Koenigstiger_Saumur_F.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koenigstiger_Saumur_F.jpg)

The working Tiger II of the *Musée des Blindés* being displayed to the public, 2005

The only working example is displayed at the *[Musée des Blindés](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_des_Blind%C3%A9s "Musée des Blindés")*, [Saumur](/wiki/Saumur "Saumur"), France. It has the production turret and is accessible to the public. This tank belonged to the 3rd Company of the schwere Panzerabteilung 503. It was believed to have been abandoned by its crew on 23 August 1944, due to engine problems, at [Brueil-en-Vexin](/wiki/Brueil-en-Vexin "Brueil-en-Vexin"), near [Mantes-la-Jolie](/wiki/Mantes-la-Jolie "Mantes-la-Jolie"). It was salvaged by the French Army in September 1944 and then stored in a factory in [Satory](/wiki/Satory "Satory") before being transferred to the museum in 1975. It was believed to have had turret number 123, but Colonel Michel Aubry, the founder of the museum, decided to put 233 on the turret in honour of the Tiger II that destroyed his Sherman tank at the end of the war. Today the vehicle bears its original number 300, thanks to the research work of the Musée des Blindés on historical photos . Unlike other captured German vehicles, this Tiger II was never used by the French Army.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
Other survivors include:

[![A frontal view of a large, pale-yellow tank in a white museum gallery.. Its curved-faced turret is pointing forwards, the long gun overhangs the front by several meters.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Panzerkampfwagen_VI_Ausfuhrung_B_%28Tiger_II_pre-production%29_front-left2_2017_Bovington.jpg/250px-Panzerkampfwagen_VI_Ausfuhrung_B_%28Tiger_II_pre-production%29_front-left2_2017_Bovington.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panzerkampfwagen_VI_Ausfuhrung_B_(Tiger_II_pre-production)_front-left2_2017_Bovington.jpg)

The Bovington Tank Museum's prototype Tiger II on display at the museum's Tiger Collection Exhibition, 2017

* [The Tank Museum](/wiki/The_Tank_Museum "The Tank Museum"), Dorset, UK: Tiger II with early production turret is on display. This vehicle was the second soft steel prototype made and did not see active service. They have planned and started work on the restoration of making this a running and complete vehicle [[67]](#cite_note-73)[[68]](#cite_note-74)
* [Defence Academy of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Defence_Academy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Defence Academy of the United Kingdom"), [Shrivenham](/wiki/Shrivenham "Shrivenham"), UK: Tiger II (production turret). This vehicle was from *s.SS Pz.Abt. 501*, with hull number 280093, turret number 104, and has a comprehensive coating of [Zimmerit](/wiki/Zimmerit "Zimmerit").[[69]](#cite_note-75) It was claimed by Sergeant Roberts of A Squadron, [23rd Hussars](/wiki/23rd_Hussars "23rd Hussars"), [11th Armoured Division](/wiki/11th_Armoured_Division "11th Armoured Division") in a Sherman tank near [Beauvais](/wiki/Beauvais "Beauvais"), although it had already been disabled and abandoned by its crew following damage to its tracks and final drive.[[70]](#cite_note-76) This vehicle is currently on display at The Tank Museum, in Dorset, UK.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [The Wheatcroft Collection](/wiki/The_Wheatcroft_Collection "The Wheatcroft Collection"), [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire"), UK. A private collector, [Kevin Wheatcroft](/wiki/Kevin_Wheatcroft "Kevin Wheatcroft"), is about[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] to start a restoration/rebuild of a complete Tiger II. The project will include parts from many individual Tiger IIs, but many parts will be of new manufacture. Wheatcroft has stated that he has 70–80% of the original parts needed for a reconstruction and more parts are sourced continuously. Known and shown parts are a complete front glacis plate, 8.8 cm KwK 43 main armament, engine deck plates, approx. 1/3 hull (rear) in one part, a set of tracks, and approx. 2/3 of the left-side hull plate in two parts.[[71]](#cite_note-77) The aim of the project is a complete Tiger II in running order.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![A three quarters view of a large tank with a flat-faced turret, dull yellow, green and brown wavy camouflage, on display inside a museum. The frontal armour is sloped. The long gun overhangs the bow by several meters. Two waist-high cartridges sit on their bases in front of it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/SdKfz182.jpg/250px-SdKfz182.jpg)](/wiki/File:SdKfz182.jpg)

Tiger II with the production turret, at the *[Deutsches Panzermuseum](/wiki/Deutsches_Panzermuseum "Deutsches Panzermuseum")*, Germany

* [Deutsches Panzermuseum](/wiki/Deutsches_Panzermuseum "Deutsches Panzermuseum"), [Munster](/wiki/Munster,_Lower_Saxony "Munster, Lower Saxony"), Germany: Tiger II (production turret), hull number 280101.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Originally bearing turret number 121 from s.SS.Pz.Abt 501, it was restored with a different number for unknown reasons.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Mantes-la-Jolie](/wiki/Mantes-la-Jolie "Mantes-la-Jolie"), France. A more or less complete, but wrecked, Tiger II (production turret) is buried under regional road 913. Parts of the turret were recovered in a limited exploratory excavation in 2001. Further excavation halted for financial reasons. There are plans to fully excavate and restore this Tiger II for a [Vexin](/wiki/Vexin "Vexin") battle memorial.[[72]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Tank_%22Tiger_II%22.Inscription_on_a_trunk_%22Glory_to_the_Captain_Korobov%21%22_%284567311642%29.jpg/250px-Tank_%22Tiger_II%22.Inscription_on_a_trunk_%22Glory_to_the_Captain_Korobov%21%22_%284567311642%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tank_%22Tiger_II%22.Inscription_on_a_trunk_%22Glory_to_the_Captain_Korobov!%22_(4567311642).jpg)

Tiger II at [Kubinka Tank Museum](/wiki/Kubinka_Tank_Museum "Kubinka Tank Museum")

* [Kubinka Tank Museum](/wiki/Kubinka_Tank_Museum "Kubinka Tank Museum"), Russia: Tiger II (production turret) with turret number 002 (502) captured at Oględów by the Red Army.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![The side of a large tank, freshly painted in pale yellow, green and rust-brown camouflage, sitting in sunlight on a concrete plinth.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Tiger_II_La_Gleize2008.jpg/250px-Tiger_II_La_Gleize2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiger_II_La_Gleize2008.jpg)

Tiger II at La Gleize, Belgium

* [December 44 Museum](/wiki/La_Gleize#Sights "La Gleize"), [La Gleize](/wiki/La_Gleize "La Gleize"), Belgium: A cosmetically restored Tiger II (production turret), hull number 280273, built in October 1944. Turret number 213 from *s.SS Pz.Abt 501*. Displayed at the entrance to December 44 Museum Collections, a museum devoted entirely to the [Battle of the Bulge](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Bulge "Battle of the Bulge"). This tank was abandoned in La Gleize on 24 December 1944, where the advance of [Kampfgruppe Peiper](/wiki/Kampfgruppe_Peiper "Kampfgruppe Peiper") was halted. The front part, about 1/3, of the gun barrel is restored with a Panther gun barrel and muzzle brake.[[73]](#cite_note-79) It also has restored mudguards. It is stripped of exterior and internal fittings and most of the torsion bars are broken, but it still has its gearbox and engine in place.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [U.S. Army Armor & Cavalry Collection](/wiki/U.S._Army_Armor_%26_Cavalry_Collection "U.S. Army Armor & Cavalry Collection"), [Fort Benning](/wiki/Fort_Benning "Fort Benning"), Georgia, United States: Tiger II (production turret), hull number 280243, built in September 1944. Turret number 332 from *s.SS Pz.Abt. 501*. Captured during the [Battle of the Bulge](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Bulge "Battle of the Bulge") by Sgt. Glenn D. George of the 740th Tank Battalion of the 1st US Army on December 24, 1944.[[74]](#cite_note-80) The left side was cut open for educational purposes at the [Aberdeen Proving Ground](/wiki/Aberdeen_Proving_Ground "Aberdeen Proving Ground") in the late 1940s. Was on display at the former "Patton Museum of Cavalry & Armor, Fort Knox KY, then under BRAC transferred to Fort Benning.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Schweizerisches Militärmuseum Full](/wiki/Schweizerisches_Milit%C3%A4rmuseum_Full "Schweizerisches Militärmuseum Full"), Switzerland. This Tiger II (production turret) was previously displayed in the Thun Tank Museum, and was loaned to the Schweizerisches Militärmuseum Full in September 2006). This tank was given to Switzerland by France after the war. Hull number 280215 from *s.H.Pz.Abt 506*.[[75]](#cite_note-81) As of 2021, it is in the process of being restored to working order.[[76]](#cite_note-82)
